channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: MTG Secrets of Strixhaven - New Cards Revealed!
item.content:encoded:
Class is back in session as the newest Magic: The Gathering set, Secrets of Strixhaven, preps to release later this month. I got the chance to sit in on a press preview with the Wizards of the Coast design team showing off tons of sneak peeks from the set, so follow along as I run through some seriously cool Secrets of Strixhaven new cards.

Secrets of Strixhaven New Cards Reveal Innovative Mechanics

With the upcoming release of the new set, we're treated to a full course load of new mechanics. In the press preview, we got a look at them all - so let's run through a few examples of each as we check out some new cards!

Prismari - Opus

First up is Prismari, the art school of the University that focuses on self-expression through the elements. That comes through with their Opus mechanic. No matter the incarnation of the spell, the structure is the same: you get a bonus for casting an instant or sorcery, and if you spend five or more mana you get an even bigger bonus!

I love this idea, which feels like a twist and refinement on the Bloomburrow Expend Mechanic (except you've got to pay it all on one spell), and anything that sweetens the pot when it comes to casting big instant and sorcery spells sounds good to me!

Silverquill - Repartee

Whether witty or not, Silverquill's Repartee mechanic triggers special bonuses whenever you cast an instant or sorcery that targets a creature. Fun, easy to digest, and I love the way this reflects Silverquill's focus on verbal magic. 

When you directly target another creature, your acerbic wit is so potent you can't help but gain a boon from it. From the cards we see here, this could be one of the most powerful keywords of the set, and it'll be sure to lead to some spicy limited moments as it encourages players to build around interaction.

Witherbloom - Infusion

The nature mages of Witherbloom College are best summed up by their motto: Get Your Hands Dirty. And their new Infusion mechanic encourages you to embrace creation. No matter what benefit you receive, the keyword triggers "if you gained life this turn." 

These two cards with the mechanic (I paired the image above with their mascot, as we only saw two cards with Infusion) show different ways you benefit from having gained life. What's interesting here is that it's a state-based action - when you cast the spell or when the creature enters, if you've already gained life this turn you get a benefit. It's not like the mascot which gets a +1/+1 counter whenever you gain life. These are one-time shots, or so it seems from what's been previewed so far.

Quandrix - Increment

Now we're onto what might be my favorite mechanic of the set, Quandrix's Increment keyword. We only got to see two versions of this on cards, but you can see how the card in the center - their mascot - will go a long way toward triggering your Increment ability. 

Very much akin to one of my other favorite mechanics, Simic's Evolve, Increment says that whenever you cast a spell, if it cost more than the power or toughness of the creature with increment, you give that creature a +1/+1 counter. This is extremely clever game design, as it rewards players who are casting spells perfectly on curve - something we'd expect from the brainy and numbers-obsessed Quandrix College.

Lorehold - Flashback

Of course the history-obsessed Lorehold College's special mechanic for the set would be... Flashback, an old mechanic brought back to the game. That's honestly... very funny. Only Magic: The Gathering has the design space to do something like this - where the return of an old mechanic actually feels very in-universe, and like a subtle inside joke, as well!

And while we've seen Flashback in a few cards across Lorwyn Eclipsed, Avatar, and Final Fantasy, it's always nice to see it front and center here. And while we only got one preview card with the mechanic itself, you'll notice the two other cards I grouped with it, which care about cards leaving your graveyard. That's pretty rare in W/R!

The Mechanic Everyone's Talking About With These Secrets of Strixhaven New Cards...

Let's be real, you don't write the words "Ancestral Recall" on a Magic: The Gathering card in the year of our Lord Urza 2026 and expect people not to sit up and take notice. How do we bring restricted, power 9 level cards back to the game without breaking it? Those mad professors have done it again with the Prepare mechanic.

Woo hoo this is spicy! Creatures with the Prepare keyword show up in these dual-bottomed card frames (like Adventures and Omens), on the left you'll see any relevant stats for the creatures, and on the right in their own little box, with its own mana cost, you'll see the spell they need to "prepare" to cast. Here's how it works: you cast the creature card, and sometimes it enters "Prepared" while other times you'll need to find a way to make it "Prepared." 

Once it's "Prepared" you can then cast the spell on its box (following the mana cost and any timing restrictions). You can't be prepared more than once, too - it's either an on or off switch. But once you cast the prepare spell, you become unprepared, and you'll need to prepare again to make it happen once more. Pretty tricky, and kind of weird to write out like this, but I think it intrinsically makes sense. Though keeping track of whether a creature is prepared or not could be tricky on the tabletop.

Secrets of Strixhaven New Cards Include New Planeswalkers And Elder Dragons, Too!

Can you believe that we're getting cards for each of the five Elder Dragons who serve as the founders and catalysts for each of the five colleges? I'm so excited, and a already dreaming about which commander decks I want to build out of these (Quandrix is calling me).

Do you have a favorite Elder Dragon from this batch? I think they're all pretty impressive, and could easily get out of hand quickly on the battlefield. These will certainly be draft-around bombs in limited, and I can see them having a big impact on Commander as well.

Wait a minute - what in the world is Ral doing as a mono-Black card!? We love a guest lecturer, and I'm really impressed with how these card abilities ladder up to a classic White/Black strategy, but still feel random like we'd expect from our chaotic boy Ral. And as for Dellian Fel, the newcomer to Witherbloom College, there's just so much utility on display here. He can trigger your Infusion with his +2, nobody hates card draw, or creature destruction, and his ultimate is really a game ender.

Secrets of Strixhaven New Cards Also Feature New Commanders

In closing, I'll leave you with some photos of the five new commanders who are each helming their own Commander deck. I'm so excited about this, as it's been a while since we got a full suite of five commander decks for a set. And each of these featured commanders look really fun and unique in their own ways.

Secrets of Strixhaven New Cards Also Include Bonus Sheet Cards!

Suprise! Bonus Sheet time! 

Last but not least, I'll just leave you with the beauty of these bonus sheet cards. Which ones of these are you most excited to try and rip? Let me know in the comments below!

Giaco Furino joined the TechRaptor team as a Staff Writer in 2019 after searching for a dedicated place to write and talk about Tabletop Games. In 2020, he… More about Giaco
